description: Greenstar Si Compact, Greenstar i, Greenstar 4000 and CDi Classic ranges. Codes cover combi, system and regular models.

faultCodes.title: Pump running dry or seized
faultCodes.summary: Boiler detects the pump is circulating no water, either because the pump is seized or the system is air-locked or empty.
likelyCauses: System pressure too low or empty
Pump shaft seized (magnetic rotor stuck after long idle period)
Auto air vent blocked or capped
quickChecks: Check system pressure — should be 1.0–1.5 bar cold
Listen for pump running when boiler fires
Bleed radiators and auto air vent on top of pump
Free a seized pump by loosening the centre screw and rotating the shaft with a flat screwdriver

faultCodes.title: Hot water NTC sensor defective
faultCodes.summary: The domestic hot water temperature sensor is reading out of range.
likelyCauses: NTC sensor open or short circuit
quickChecks: Unplug and re-seat the DHW NTC connector
Measure NTC resistance against the standard table — should fall between 8k–12kΩ at room temperature
Inspect the loom for damage near the heat exchanger

faultCodes.title: Communication break with FX or remote controls
faultCodes.summary: The PCB has lost communication with a connected Worcester room-stat, FX programmer or weather-compensation controller.
likelyCauses: Broken or shorted data cable to the controller
quickChecks: Check continuity of the two data wires between boiler and controller
Power-cycle the boiler and controller
Swap in a known-good controller if one is available

faultCodes.title: Code plug missing or not detected
faultCodes.summary: The boiler cannot read the coding plug that tells the PCB which model and output it is controlling.
likelyCauses: Coding plug removed during a service
Damaged or wrong coding plug after a PCB swap
quickChecks: Turn boiler off at the spur, re-seat the coding plug firmly, and power back on
Confirm the coding plug matches the model rating plate
Check for broken pins on the plug

faultCodes.title: Fan speed too low
faultCodes.summary: The fan is not reaching the speed the PCB expects, either on start-up or during operation.
likelyCauses: Blocked flue or heavy condensate in the flue
quickChecks: Listen and feel the fan on start-up — rough bearings are obvious
Inspect flue terminal for blockage (nests, debris)
Drain condensate from the flue low point if fitted
Check 230V supply to the fan and the tacho signal back to the PCB

faultCodes.title: Return sensor temperature fault
faultCodes.summary: The central heating return NTC is reading an implausible value.
quickChecks: Re-seat the return NTC plug
Measure resistance against the NTC table
Check for water ingress at the sensor

faultCodes.title: Central heating flow NTC defective
faultCodes.summary: The primary flow sensor is open, shorted or wildly out of range.
likelyCauses: Flow NTC open or short circuit
quickChecks: Unplug, inspect and re-seat the flow NTC
Measure resistance at room temperature (~10kΩ typical)
Compare reading on the service menu with actual pipe temperature

faultCodes.title: Flow temperature safety limiter tripped
faultCodes.summary: The flow thermistor or overheat stat has seen the heat exchanger rise above the safe limit.
likelyCauses: Poor circulation (seized pump, airlock, closed valves)
Limescale or sludge in the primary heat exchanger
quickChecks: Confirm pump is circulating and system is vented
Check inhibitor level and inspect for sludge (black water on expansion vessel drain)
Feel flow and return pipes — large temperature differential points to poor flow
Swap the overheat stat if it reads open cold

faultCodes.title: Flame not detected on ignition
faultCodes.summary: The boiler tried to light but the ionisation current never confirmed a flame.
likelyCauses: Gas supply isolated or emergency control valve off
Condensate trap or pipework blocked, drowning the burner
Ignition or flame-sense electrode dirty, cracked or out of gap
Ignition lead perished or earthing out
quickChecks: Check gas is on and other gas appliances run normally
Confirm mains polarity with a socket tester or meter
Clean the flame-sense electrode and check the spark gap (typically 3–4 mm)
Clear and reseat the condensate trap, prove it is draining
Verify gas inlet working pressure at the test point

faultCodes.title: False flame detected when boiler is off
faultCodes.summary: The PCB is seeing a flame signal when the gas valve is closed.
quickChecks: Dry and inspect the ignition lead and electrode
Check the electrode porcelain for fine cracks
Isolate and watch the burner — gas escaping when off points to a passing gas valve

faultCodes.title: Internal PCB error
faultCodes.summary: The PCB has detected an internal software or hardware fault.
quickChecks: Turn the boiler off for 60 seconds and back on
Inspect the PCB for water staining, green corrosion or burnt components
Measure mains voltage under load

faultCodes.title: Flame detected while boiler is off
faultCodes.summary: Similar to EB — the boiler sees a flame signal when there should be none.
likelyCauses: Gas valve passing gas when closed
quickChecks: Dry out the burner chamber and lead
Inspect electrode for cracks
Soap-test the burner when the gas valve is closed

faultCodes.title: Flame detected after gas shut off
faultCodes.summary: After the gas valve closed at end of cycle, the PCB still sees flame — usually a gas valve passing.
quickChecks: Soap test the burner with the boiler off to confirm valve closure
Dry and inspect the ignition lead
Replace the gas valve if it is proven to be passing

faultCodes.title: Reset button pressed in error
faultCodes.summary: The boiler logs a reset that was pressed without a prior lockout.
quickChecks: No action needed — clears automatically

faultCodes.title: Low system pressure warning
faultCodes.summary: Pressure has dropped close to the low cut-off but the boiler is still running.
quickChecks: Top up the system to 1.2 bar cold
Walk radiators and visible pipework for weeping joints
Check the PRV outside discharge pipe for dripping

faultCodes.title: Waiting for airflow
faultCodes.summary: The air pressure switch or fan tacho has not confirmed airflow yet at start-up.
quickChecks: Confirm fan is actually spinning on start-up
Inspect the flue run for debris
Re-seat the fan connector and tacho lead

faultCodes.title: System pressure too low
faultCodes.summary: Pressure sensor reads below the minimum operating threshold.
quickChecks: Top up to 1.2 bar cold
Check pressure sensor reading against the gauge
Pressurise expansion vessel to 1.0 bar with system drained

faultCodes.title: Flow temperature too high
faultCodes.summary: Primary flow temperature has exceeded the PCB limit, often alongside poor circulation.
quickChecks: Check pump operation and system pressure
Vent rads and auto air vent
Inspect heat exchanger for sludge evidence

faultCodes.title: Fan running too fast
faultCodes.summary: Fan overshoot — PCB commanding a speed but the tacho reports much higher RPM.
quickChecks: Check fan tacho wiring and connector
Run the boiler in service test to confirm commanded vs actual speed

faultCodes.title: Fan running too slow
faultCodes.summary: Fan not reaching commanded RPM.
quickChecks: Spin the fan by hand when isolated — grinding or stiff = bearings
Check supply voltage to the fan
Clean the fan impeller if accessible

faultCodes.title: Fan start-up problem
faultCodes.summary: Fan failed to spin up within the allowed window on a call for heat.
quickChecks: Confirm 230V supply at the fan connector on call for heat
Spin the fan manually and listen for bearing noise
Re-seat all fan connections

faultCodes.title: Primary flow to return differential too large
faultCodes.summary: The gap between flow and return temperatures exceeds the safe threshold, usually indicating starved circulation.
likelyCauses: Blocked or limed primary heat exchanger
quickChecks: Confirm pump is running at normal speed
Check all isolation valves are fully open
Inspect heat exchanger flow passages if accessible

faultCodes.title: Flow temperature rising too quickly
faultCodes.summary: Rate of change on the flow NTC is above the expected limit — points to poor flow past the sensor.
quickChecks: Bleed the auto air vent
Spin the pump shaft by hand if cold
Verify all isolation valves are fully open

faultCodes.title: Mains power error / electrical interruption
faultCodes.summary: The boiler has detected a mains power interruption or spike. This can trip the boiler into lockout.
likelyCauses: Unstable mains supply or power cut
Loose wiring at spur or plug
Faulty PCB unable to regulate power
quickChecks: Check mains voltage at the boiler spur — should be 230V +/-
Inspect wiring at fused spur for loose connections
If power stable, reset — if it recurs, suspect PCB

faultCodes.title: Temperature differential exceeding limit
faultCodes.summary: The flow and return (or flow and safety sensor) temperature difference is too large, indicating a circulation issue.
likelyCauses: Pump not running at correct speed or failing
Faulty NTC sensor giving incorrect reading
quickChecks: Check pump is running and speed is adequate
Feel the flow and return pipes — large temp difference confirms circulation issue
Check for system blockage — flush if needed

faultCodes.title: Fan stopped during operation
faultCodes.summary: The fan has stopped spinning mid-operation. The boiler locks out to prevent incomplete combustion.
quickChecks: Listen for the fan — is it spinning at all when the boiler fires?
Check the fan connector is fully plugged in
Measure voltage at fan leads — should see ~240V AC when demanded

faultCodes.title: Gas valve coil disconnected
faultCodes.summary: The boiler has detected that the gas valve coil wiring is disconnected or has an open circuit.
likelyCauses: Loose connector on gas valve coil
Broken wire in gas valve harness
quickChecks: Check gas valve connector is fully seated
Measure gas valve coil resistance — typically 30-60Ω per coil
Inspect harness for damaged or broken wires

faultCodes.title: No pressure change detected after pump activation
faultCodes.summary: The pressure sensor detected no pressure change when the pump was activated, suggesting a pump or sensor fault.
likelyCauses: Very low system pressure (below sensor range)
quickChecks: Check system pressure on the gauge — repressurise if below 1 bar
Listen for the pump — if silent, it may be seized
Tap the pump gently to free it if stuck

faultCodes.title: Flue gas sensor temperature too high
faultCodes.summary: The flue gas temperature has exceeded the safe limit. The boiler locks out to prevent damage to the heat exchanger or flue system.
likelyCauses: Blocked or restricted heat exchanger (scale/sludge build-up)
Incorrect gas rate — too much gas burning
Faulty flue gas thermistor reading high
quickChecks: Check the flue for obstructions or blockages
Run a combustion analysis — check CO/CO2 ratio
Inspect the heat exchanger for signs of scaling or blockage

faultCodes.title: Flue gas temperature rising too fast
faultCodes.summary: The flue gas temperature is rising faster than expected, indicating possible heat exchanger or burner issues.
likelyCauses: Blocked heat exchanger — sludge or scale restricting water flow
quickChecks: Check gas pressure at the burner — compare to data badge spec
Inspect heat exchanger for signs of blockage
Check flue gas sensor resistance against spec

faultCodes.title: Supply temperature sensor exceeded 110°C
faultCodes.summary: The flow temperature safety sensor has detected a temperature above 110°C. This is a critical safety shutdown.
likelyCauses: System circulation blocked — pump failure or system sludge
quickChecks: Check pump is running — is water circulating?
Check for system blockages — feel pipes for cold spots
If pump OK and water flowing, suspect sensor or heat exchanger

faultCodes.title: Communication to pump has failed
faultCodes.summary: The boiler cannot communicate with the pump. On Greenstar models with intelligent pump control, this means the pump data link has failed.
likelyCauses: Loose wiring between PCB and pump
quickChecks: Check wires to pump are secure — reconnect and re-power
Verify the correct pump type is fitted for the model
Reset the boiler and monitor

faultCodes.title: DHW temperature sensor defect
faultCodes.summary: The domestic hot water outlet temperature sensor is showing an incorrect or out-of-range reading.
likelyCauses: Wiring damage between sensor and PCB
quickChecks: Check DHW sensor wiring and connector for damage
Measure sensor resistance — should be ~10kΩ at 25°C
If resistance is wrong, replace the sensor

faultCodes.title: Outdoor temperature sensor defective
faultCodes.summary: The outdoor temperature sensor is reading incorrectly or is disconnected. The boiler will operate without weather compensation.
likelyCauses: Outdoor sensor disconnected or cable damaged
Sensor exposed to direct sunlight giving false readings
quickChecks: Check the outdoor sensor wiring and connections at the boiler
Verify sensor is positioned on a north-facing wall, out of direct sunlight
Measure sensor resistance — compare to manufacturer's chart

faultCodes.title: Ionisation sensor shorted
faultCodes.summary: The ionisation (flame sensing) electrode has a short circuit to earth, preventing flame detection.
likelyCauses: Ionisation lead touching burner or metalwork
Cracked or damaged ionisation electrode insulator
quickChecks: Inspect the ionisation electrode and lead for cracks or contact with metal parts
Clean the electrode tip and check for carbon deposits
Measure electrode resistance to earth — should be open circuit when cold

flowcharts.title: Hot water flow sensor — voltage test
safetyWarnings: 230V present on adjacent terminals — take care with probes
Leave cover off only as long as needed, refit securely afterwards

steps.body: Isolate gas. Remove the front cover and drop the control box cover. Identify the flow sensor connector on the cold inlet — typically three wires (red, black, yellow).
Set the multimeter to DC volts (20V range). Back-probe the connector while it remains plugged into the PCB.
Probe the supply rail. Put the red lead on the red wire and the black lead on the black wire. This is the 5V supply from the PCB to the sensor.
Now check the signal line. Move the red lead to the yellow wire, keeping the black lead on the black wire. With no water flowing it should sit near one rail.
steps.expected: Close to 5V or close to 0V at rest

steps.body: Open a hot tap fully. The flow sensor impeller should spin and pulse the signal line, so the meter now sees an average around mid-rail.
With the hot tap running, read the yellow-to-black voltage again.
steps.expected: Averages around 2.5V DC while water is flowing

steps.body: No 5V supply from the PCB to the sensor. Check the connector for water ingress and wiring continuity back to the PCB. If supply cable is good, the PCB flow-sensor circuit has failed.
Sensor is powered correctly but not pulsing on flow. The flow sensor has failed and should be replaced.
Supply and signal are healthy. Sensor is working. If hot water still fails, move on to the diverter valve, secondary heat exchanger or DHW NTC.

flowcharts.title: No ignition lockout (EA / ignition fault)
safetyWarnings: Prove gas isolation before opening combustion chamber

steps.body: Are other gas appliances in the property working normally?

options.label: No, no gas at other appliances

steps.body: Reset the boiler and watch/listen. You should hear the fan purge, then ignition sparks, then the burner either lights or locks out.
Are you hearing ignition sparks?
Does the burner light at all before locking out?
Burner lighting but dropping = flame not being sensed. Inspect the flame-sense electrode for soot, cracks or incorrect gap. Check the lead and earth path.
Burner not lighting despite sparks = no gas arriving at the burner, or gas valve not opening. Check inlet working pressure with a manometer at the test point while calling for heat.
Measure inlet gas pressure at the boiler test point with the appliance calling for heat.
steps.expected: Approximately 20 mbar natural gas, 37 mbar LPG (within manufacturer tolerance)

steps.body: Gas supply issue upstream of the boiler. Check emergency control valve, meter, and any in-line shut-offs. If pressure is low, investigate the supply pipe sizing or a partially closed valve.
No sparks. Check the ignition lead for damage, the electrode gap, and the PCB ignition output. A cracked electrode or perished lead is the most common culprit.
Flame is being made but not detected. Clean or replace the flame-sense electrode, check the earth path back to the PCB, and verify the ionisation current in service mode.
Gas arriving at the boiler but not reaching the burner. Check the gas valve coil resistance and that 230V is being delivered to the valve on call for heat. Replace gas valve if proven faulty.

flowcharts.title: Low system pressure
safetyWarnings: Isolate power before working on the expansion vessel
Hot water in the system — let it cool before draining

steps.body: Top the system up to 1.2 bar cold and note the time. Ask the customer when they last needed to top up.
Has the pressure been dropping over weeks, or quickly (within days)?
Gradual drops are typically the expansion vessel. Isolate power. Drain the system down below the vessel (around 0 bar on the gauge).
With the system drained, check the expansion vessel air charge through the Schrader valve.
Walk every visible radiator valve, pipe joint and the boiler internals looking for weeps. Check the PRV discharge pipe outside for any drip.
Found a visible leak?

options.label: Yes, found a wet joint or valve

steps.body: Expansion vessel has lost its air charge. Re-pressurise to spec or replace the vessel if it has failed internally (water at the Schrader valve = ruptured diaphragm).
Repair the identified leak, refill, vent and re-pressurise to 1.2 bar. Monitor for 24 hours.
No visible leak and vessel is good. Suspect a buried leak under floors or screed. Consider a pressure test with the boiler isolated, or a trace leak dye.
PRV is dumping water. Either the expansion vessel has failed (most common), the PRV itself has debris on the seat, or the filling loop was left open. Check vessel first, then PRV.

flowcharts.title: No central heating, hot water works

steps.body: Does the boiler fire on a hot tap but stay silent on a heating demand?
When heating calls, does the pump run?

options.label: Pump runs, boiler does not fire

steps.body: Diverter valve may be stuck on DHW. Cycle heating a few times and feel the valve body — you should hear/feel the actuator move.
Does the diverter actuator move when heating calls?
Boiler isn't firing for either mode. This is a different fault — start with the no-ignition flowchart.
No demand signal reaching the boiler. Check stat wiring, programmer state, and that the heating select is on. Bridge the CH demand terminals temporarily to confirm.
Diverter actuator not moving on CH call. Check 230V to the actuator, then the motor/cartridge. Replace actuator or diverter cartridge as required.
Actuator moves but boiler does not fire on CH. Check flow NTC and overheat stat readings in service mode, and verify the primary circulation. If flow sensor reads a high temp at rest, replace it.

flowcharts.title: Fan fault (C6 / 265 / 266 / 283)
safetyWarnings: Isolate mains before unplugging fan connections

steps.body: Drop the case and power the boiler. Call for heat and watch the fan directly.
Does the fan spin up at all?
Smooth spin but still locking out. This points to the tacho signal rather than the motor.
Isolate power and unplug the fan. Measure the two winding wires.
Check resistance across the fan winding pins.
steps.expected: Typically 10–80 Ω depending on model (must match the service manual figure)

steps.body: Bearings worn — fan assembly needs replacing.
Fan spins but PCB not seeing tacho. Inspect the tacho/signal wire continuity. If the wiring is good, replace the fan assembly (integrated tacho).
Winding open — motor failed. Replace fan.
Fan winding healthy but not spinning. Measure 230V supply at the fan connector on call for heat. If absent, inspect the loom and PCB fan driver.

flowcharts.title: Frozen condensate pipe
toolsNeeded: Hot water jug or hot water bottle
safetyWarnings: Do not use boiling water on plastic condensate pipe — warm tap water only

steps.body: External condensate pipe outside? Cold weather and boiler locked out with EA or similar?

options.label: Yes, pipe outside in a frost event

steps.body: Locate the external section. Feel for an ice plug — typically at the base of a vertical section, at an elbow, or inside the external termination.
Lay a warm cloth or pour warm (not boiling) tap water gently over the suspected ice plug. Work down from the top. Listen for water starting to move.
Reset the boiler. It should now run normally.
Advise the customer: re-route the condensate internally, upsize the pipe to 32mm minimum externally, insulate thoroughly, or fit a trace heater. Quote as required.
Condensate routed internally, so freezing is unlikely to be the issue. Check the internal trap for blockage and clear if needed.
Pipe thawed, boiler running. Recommend an upgrade to prevent recurrence.

flowcharts.title: PRV leaking / excess pressure
safetyWarnings: Isolate the boiler flow and return before draining down. Do not attempt expansion vessel recharge with the system pressurised.
Always follow the manufacturer's instructions for the correct expansion vessel pre-charge pressure — typically 0.5–1.0 bar.

steps.body: Isolate the boiler flow and return. Open the boiler drain-off valve to de-pressurise the system to atmospheric pressure.
With the system at atmospheric pressure, attach a tyre pressure gauge to the expansion vessel Schrader valve. What is the pre-charge pressure?
Re-charge the expansion vessel to the manufacturer's specified pressure using a pump. Leave connected for 5 minutes to ensure pressure holds. Spray leak detection fluid on the Schrader valve.
Does the expansion vessel hold the recharged pressure for 5 minutes without dropping?
Re-pressurise the boiler, open flow and return. Fire the boiler on heating and let it reach full temperature. Monitor the pressure gauge.
Does the pressure still rise above 3 bar or is the PRV still discharging while the boiler heats up?

options.label: No — pressure stays in normal range

steps.body: Disconnect the filling loop or check that it is fully closed. With the filling loop disconnected, does the pressure still rise while the boiler is cold/idle?

options.label: Yes — still rising with filling loop off
No — pressure stable after disconnecting filling loop

steps.body: The expansion vessel diaphragm has failed and cannot hold pre-charge pressure. Replace the expansion vessel. Also recommend replacing the PRV as good practice on any pressure fault.

Expansion vessel recharge resolved the issue. The PRV was discharging because the flat vessel couldn't absorb system expansion. Monitor over the next few days. Recommend replacing the PRV as good practice.
The plate heat exchanger has an internal leak allowing mains water pressure into the heating circuit. Replace the plate heat exchanger. Also recommend replacing the PRV.
The filling loop was passing — letting mains pressure into the sealed system. Replace or properly close the filling loop. On some models, check for an integral filling link that may need replacing.

flowcharts.title: No hot water — heating works fine
safetyWarnings: Isolate the electrical supply before testing any wiring. Take care around hot pipework.

steps.body: Turn on a hot tap and observe the boiler. Does the boiler attempt to fire for hot water (fan starts, ignition attempts)?

options.label: Yes — boiler fires but water stays cold
No — boiler doesn't respond to hot tap demand

steps.body: The boiler is firing but hot water isn't getting through. Feel the pipework — is the flow to the hot water outlet warm, but the actual hot water cold or lukewarm?

options.label: Flow pipe warm, outlet cold — diverter valve not switching
Everything heating up but water still lukewarm

steps.body: If the water is lukewarm, the plate heat exchanger may be blocked or there's a flow rate issue. Has the boiler been descaled recently? Is the incoming mains pressure adequate?

options.label: Poor mains flow — low water pressure at taps
Mains flow OK — suspect blocked plate

steps.body: The boiler doesn't recognise a hot water demand. Does the boiler show any fault codes on the display?

options.label: No fault code — boiler just ignores the hot tap

steps.body: Check the flow sensor / flow switch. Open a hot tap and listen — can you hear the turbine in the flow sensor spinning? On Greenstar models, check for 1–6V at the flow sensor connector.

options.label: No signal / no spin — flow sensor faulty

steps.body: The diverter valve is not switching from heating to hot water mode. On most combis this is a motorised valve inside the boiler. Check for 240V at the diverter motor when hot water is demanded. If power present but valve not moving, replace the diverter valve or motor cartridge.

Low incoming mains water pressure. The boiler needs adequate flow to trigger DHW mode. Check the mains stopcock is fully open. You may need a mains pressure boost or a plumber to address supply issues.
The secondary (plate) heat exchanger is likely blocked with limescale, especially in hard water areas. It can sometimes be flushed with descaler, but often needs replacing.
The boiler is showing a fault code — look up the specific code for this brand in the Fault Finder for targeted diagnosis.
The DHW flow sensor / turbine is not registering water flow when a tap is opened. Replace the flow sensor. On Worcester Greenstar models, check the sensor connector for corrosion.

The flow sensor is sending a signal but the PCB isn't recognising the demand. Possible PCB fault. Try resetting the boiler first. If it persists, the PCB may need replacing — contact the manufacturer.

flowcharts.title: Boiler kettling or banging noises
toolsNeeded: Stethoscope or ear against pipe (careful — hot!)
safetyWarnings: Pipework may be very hot — use caution when touching or listening. Never put your ear directly on hot pipes.

steps.body: When does the noise occur? Fire the boiler on heating and listen carefully.

options.label: Continuous rumbling/kettling while the burner is on
Banging from pipes, not the boiler itself

steps.body: Kettling during operation usually means limescale build-up in the heat exchanger or inadequate circulation. Has the system ever been flushed or treated with inhibitor?

options.label: No — never flushed, old system, likely sludge/scale

steps.body: Check the pump speed and circulation. Is the pump running at the correct speed? Are all radiator valves open? Is there a good flow/return temperature difference (10–20°C)?

options.label: Delta T too high (>25°C) — poor circulation
Circulation seems fine — delta T normal

steps.body: Banging on ignition is typically delayed ignition — gas builds up before the spark catches. Check the electrode gap and condition, clean the burner, and verify gas pressure at the burner. If the burner is clogged, clean or replace it.

Banging from pipework (not the boiler) is usually thermal expansion. Pipes expanding against joists or floorboards. Check pipe clips and add felt or lagging where pipes pass through tight spaces. Not a boiler fault.

Limescale and sludge build-up in the heat exchanger is the most common cause of kettling. Recommend a full system powerflush with a suitable chemical inhibitor added afterwards. In severe cases, the heat exchanger may need replacing.

Poor circulation is causing localised overheating in the heat exchanger (kettling). Check pump is on correct speed, all valves are open, and there are no blockages. A system flush may be needed. Consider fitting a magnetic filter.

If circulation is fine but kettling persists, the primary heat exchanger may have internal scaling that flushing couldn't remove. In hard water areas this is common. The heat exchanger may need replacing or the boiler descaled with a specialist chemical.
